title: Study Shows Fasting Mimicking Diet Improves Crohn's Disease Symptoms
summary: Researchers from Stanford Medicine conducted a national randomized controlled trial to investigate the effects of a fasting mimicking diet on patients with mild-to-moderate Crohn's disease. The study involved 97 participants, with 65 following the diet and 32 serving as a control group. Over three months, those on the diet reduced their calorie intake to 700-1,100 calories per day for five consecutive days each month, consuming plant-based meals, and then returned to their normal diet. Results showed that approximately two-thirds of the diet group reported symptom improvement, compared to fewer than half in the control group. Biological markers also indicated reduced inflammation. This research addresses a gap in dietary studies for inflammatory bowel diseases, offering evidence to support clinical recommendations. The findings, published in Nature Medicine, highlight the potential of short-term calorie restriction as a non-pharmacological intervention, especially since current treatments like steroids have significant side effects.

background_read: Crohn's disease is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease affecting about one million Americans, characterized by inflammation in the digestive tract leading to symptoms like diarrhea, cramping, and weight loss. Treatment options are limited, with steroids being the primary approved treatment for mild cases, but they carry risks of serious side effects with long-term use. Dietary management has been underexplored due to challenges in conducting rigorous studies, such as accurate reporting and placebo effects. This study's focus on a fasting mimicking diet represents an effort to provide evidence-based alternatives, addressing patient curiosity and clinical needs for non-drug interventions in managing chronic conditions.
Article_Structure: This article presents a randomized controlled trial by Stanford Medicine researchers on a fasting mimicking diet for Crohn's disease patients. Main Points include the diet's effectiveness in improving symptoms and reducing inflammation, with two-thirds of participants reporting benefits. Purpose is to fill a research gap in dietary studies for inflammatory bowel diseases and offer evidence for clinical guidance. Evidence Evaluation shows strong results from a controlled trial, though challenges like self-reporting and placebo effects are noted. Author Credibility is supported by involvement from medical institutions and publication in Nature Medicine. Methodology involved a three-month trial with 97 patients, using calorie restriction and plant-based meals, comparing outcomes to a control group.

perspectives.description: Healthcare providers may use this evidence to recommend dietary interventions as part of comprehensive care for Crohn's disease.
